title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: sobol package provides fast efficient implementation Sobol sequences quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) methods. Sobol sequences low-discrepancy sequences provide better coverage unit hypercube compared pseudo-random numbers, making valuable numerical integration, sensitivity analysis, simulation studies.

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: Sobol sequences introduced Ilya M. Sobol 1967 method generating quasi-random points low discrepancy. implementation package based : Bratley, P., & Fox, B. L. (1988). “Algorithm 659: Implementing Sobol’s quasirandom sequence generator.” ACM Transactions Mathematical Software, 14(1), 88-100. DOI: 10.1145/42288.214372 Joe, S., & Kuo, F. Y. (2008). “Constructing Sobol sequences better two-dimensional projections.” SIAM Journal Scientific Computing, 30(5), 2635-2654. DOI: 10.1145/1358628.1358630 direction numbers used implementation come Joe Kuo’s work, ensures good two-dimensional projections Property enforcement.

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: package provides simple interface generating Sobol sequences. Let’s start basic example:
code: library(sobol)  # Create a 3-dimensional Sobol generator gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 3)  # Generate a single point point <- sobol_next(gen) print(point) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Generate multiple points points <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 5) print(points) #>       [,1]  [,2]  [,3] #> [1,] 0.500 0.500 0.500 #> [2,] 0.750 0.250 0.250 #> [3,] 0.250 0.750 0.750 #> [4,] 0.375 0.625 0.125 #> [5,] 0.875 0.125 0.625

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: package supports incremental point generation, useful adaptive algorithms:
code: # Create a generator gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2)  # Generate points one at a time for (i in 1:5) {   point <- sobol_next(gen)   cat("Point", i, ":", point, "\n") } #> Point 1 : 0 0  #> Point 2 : 0.5 0.5  #> Point 3 : 0.75 0.25  #> Point 4 : 0.25 0.75  #> Point 5 : 0.375 0.625  # Check current index current_idx <- sobol_index(gen) cat("Current index:", current_idx, "\n") #> Current index: 5

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: reproducibility parallel processing, can skip point sequence:
code: # Create a generator starting from index 100 gen1 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2, skip = 100) point1 <- sobol_next(gen1)  # Or skip to a specific index gen2 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2) sobol_skip_to(gen2, 100) point2 <- sobol_next(gen2)  # These should be identical print(all.equal(point1, point2)) #> [1] TRUE

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: large-scale simulations, generate many points :
code: # Generate 1000 points at once gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2) points <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 1000)  # Visualize the coverage plot(points[, 1], points[, 2],   pch = 20, cex = 0.5,   main = "Sobol Sequence Coverage (2D)",   xlab = "Dimension 1", ylab = "Dimension 2" )

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: One key advantages Sobol sequences superior coverage sampling space:  Notice Sobol sequence provides uniform coverage without clustering.
code: # Generate Sobol points gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2) sobol_points <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 1000)  # Generate random points random_points <- matrix(runif(2000), ncol = 2)  # Plot comparison par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) plot(sobol_points[, 1], sobol_points[, 2],   pch = 20, cex = 0.5,   main = "Sobol Sequence (n=1000)",   xlab = "Dimension 1", ylab = "Dimension 2" )  plot(random_points[, 1], random_points[, 2],   pch = 20, cex = 0.5,   main = "Random Sampling (n=1000)",   xlab = "Dimension 1", ylab = "Dimension 2" ) par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: Sobol sequences can significantly improve accuracy Monte Carlo integration:
code: # Function to integrate: f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2 over [0,1]^2 # True value: 2/3 true_value <- 2 / 3  # Monte Carlo integration using random sampling mc_random <- function(n) {   points <- matrix(runif(2 * n), ncol = 2)   mean(points[, 1]^2 + points[, 2]^2) }  # Quasi-Monte Carlo integration using Sobol sequences qmc_sobol <- function(n) {   gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2)   points <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = n)   mean(points[, 1]^2 + points[, 2]^2) }  # Compare convergence sample_sizes <- c(10, 50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000) random_errors <- sapply(sample_sizes, function(n) abs(mc_random(n) - true_value)) sobol_errors <- sapply(sample_sizes, function(n) abs(qmc_sobol(n) - true_value))  # Plot convergence plot(sample_sizes, random_errors,   type = "b", log = "xy",   col = "red", pch = 19,   main = "Convergence Comparison",   xlab = "Sample Size (n)", ylab = "Absolute Error",   ylim = range(c(random_errors, sobol_errors)) ) lines(sample_sizes, sobol_errors, type = "b", col = "blue", pch = 19) legend("topright", c("Random", "Sobol"),   col = c("red", "blue"),   pch = 19, lty = 1 )

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: Sobol sequences maintain good properties even high dimensions:
code: # Generate points in 10 dimensions gen_10d <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 10) points_10d <- sobol_next_n(gen_10d, n = 1000)  # Check dimensions cat("Generated", nrow(points_10d), "points in", ncol(points_10d), "dimensions\n") #> Generated 1000 points in 10 dimensions  # Examine coverage in first two dimensions plot(points_10d[, 1], points_10d[, 2],   pch = 20, cex = 0.5,   main = "10D Sobol Sequence (Projection to 2D)",   xlab = "Dimension 1", ylab = "Dimension 2" )

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: package uses precomputed direction numbers 1000 dimensions, providing significant performance improvements:
code: library(microbenchmark)  # Benchmark batch generation microbenchmark(   n_100 = {     gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 10)     sobol_next_n(gen, 100)   },   n_1000 = {     gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 10)     sobol_next_n(gen, 1000)   },   n_10000 = {     gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 10)     sobol_next_n(gen, 10000)   },   times = 100 )

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: can use skip-ahead generate different portions sequence parallel:
code: library(parallel)  # Function to generate points from a specific range generate_chunk <- function(start_idx, n, dims) {   gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = dims, skip = start_idx)   sobol_next_n(gen, n = n) }  # Generate 10,000 points in parallel chunks cl <- makeCluster(4) clusterEvalQ(cl, library(sobol))  chunks <- parLapply(cl, 0:3, function(i) {   generate_chunk(start_idx = i * 2500, n = 2500, dims = 5) })  stopCluster(cl)  # Combine results all_points <- do.call(rbind, chunks) cat("Generated", nrow(all_points), "points in parallel\n")

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: Dimension Count: Use minimum number dimensions needed problem Sample Size: Sobol sequences effective sample sizes powers 2 (e.g., 1024, 2048) Skip Initial Points: applications, skipping first point (zeros) may beneficial Reproducibility: Use skip parameter ensure reproducible results

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: Sobol, . M. (1967). “distribution points cube approximate evaluation integrals.” USSR Computational Mathematics Mathematical Physics, 7(4), 86-112. Bratley, P., & Fox, B. L. (1988). “Algorithm 659: Implementing Sobol’s quasirandom sequence generator.” ACM Transactions Mathematical Software, 14(1), 88-100. Joe, S., & Kuo, F. Y. (2008). “Constructing Sobol sequences better two-dimensional projections.” SIAM Journal Scientific Computing, 30(5), 2635-2654.

title: Introduction to Sobol Sequences
text: sobol package provides robust, efficient implementation Sobol sequences suitable wide range quasi-Monte Carlo applications. incremental generation skip-ahead capabilities make particularly useful adaptive algorithms parallel processing. information, see package documentation visit GitHub repository.

title: Getting Started with sobol
code: # From GitHub devtools::install_github("alrobles/sobol") library(sobol)

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: Imagine tuning machine‑learning model three hyperparameters: learning rate [0.0001, 0.1] momentum [0, 0.99] dropout [0, 0.5] want explore space 200 well‑spread points. sobol_design() returns data frame ready fed objective function. Points exact ranges specified: design deterministic space‑filling – already big improvement simple random grid search.

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: Behind scenes calls sobol_points() generate Sobol sequence unit cube scales column bounds. Low discrepancy – points evenly distributed random. Reproducible – ’ll get exact design every time. wasted points – every evaluation adds information shape function. Try random Latin hypercube:
code: # Not run, but you can compare visual uniformity plot(design$learning_rate, design$momentum, col = "steelblue",      main = "Sobol design (200 points)")

what: 3. Going one level deeper: raw points
title: Getting Started with sobol
text: already scaling logic, need raw [0,1) points, use sobol_points() directly. sobol_points() accepts optional skip argument lets start arbitrary index – perfect parallel workers (see ).
code: raw <- sobol_points(n = 512, dim = 4) dim(raw)           # 512 rows, 4 columns #> [1] 512   4 range(raw)         # values in [0, 1) #> [1] 0.0000000 0.9980469

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: Sometimes don’t know advance many points ’ll need. Maybe want evaluate , check convergence, generate . ’s stateful generator shines. can also jump position: key parallel restart‑friendly workflows.
code: gen <- sobol_generator(dim = 3)  # Generate one point sobol_next(gen) #> [1] 0 0 0  # Generate a batch of 50 batch <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 50) dim(batch)  # 50 x 3 #> [1] 50  3  # What’s the current index? sobol_index(gen) #> [1] 51 sobol_skip_to(gen, 1000) sobol_index(gen) #> [1] 1000

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: sequences deterministic. two calls parameters always match: distribute work across multiple cores machines, assign non‑overlapping skip interval. Worker 1: points 0 – 999 Worker 2: points 1000 – 1999 Worker 3: points 2000 – 2999 (skip argument sobol_design() consideration – available, parallel designs become one‑liners.)
code: a <- sobol_design(lower = c(p = 0), upper = c(p = 1), nseq = 32) b <- sobol_design(lower = c(p = 0), upper = c(p = 1), nseq = 32) identical(a, b)   # TRUE #> [1] TRUE # Worker 1 w1 <- sobol_design(lower = c(lr = 0.0001, mom = 0, drop = 0),                    upper = c(lr = 0.1,    mom = 0.99, drop = 0.5),                    nseq = 1000)  # implicitly starts at 0  # Worker 2 (needs raw points + skip to 1000) raw2 <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dim = 3, skip = 1000) # Then scale raw2 manually, or use sobol_design in the future with a skip argument

what: 6. Advanced: chaining generators for adaptive sampling
title: Getting Started with sobol
text: generator can “rewound” time re‑evaluate segment:
code: gen <- sobol_generator(dim = 2) first_10 <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 10)  # Oops, need to re‑evaluate the first 10 with different parameters sobol_skip_to(gen, 0) replicated <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 10) identical(first_10, replicated)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: C++ engine heavily optimised. Even 1 000 000 points 10 dimensions complete second modern hardware, freeing spend time actual model. extremely high dimensions (>1000) engine falls back runtime generation – still fast, initialisation takes tick longer. Precomputed tables cover first 1000 dimensions instantly.

title: Getting Started with sobol
text: Reference – ?sobol_design, ?sobol_points, ?sobol_generator Articles – “Performance Optimisation” “Testing Strategy” package website Real‑world example – check inst/examples/usage_examples.R file ’s need start exploring parameter space smarter faster.Welcome sobol!
code: # Clean up rm(design, raw, gen, a, b, first_10, replicated)

code: @Manual{,   title = {sobol: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator},   author = {Angel Robles},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 1.0.0},   url = {https://alrobles.github.io/sobol}, }

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: efficient R implementation Sobol sequence generation quasi-Monte Carlo methods. package provides batch incremental interfaces full support reproducibility state management.

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: sobol_design(): instant parameter grid intuitive lower/upper bounds Fast C++ engine: core algorithms C++17, exposed via Rcpp Reproducibility: deterministic sequences skip‑ahead support parallel workflows Incremental Generation: generate points one‑‑one batches Batch Generation: Generate large matrices points single call Reproducibility: Skip arbitrary indices reproducible subsequences Full Documentation: Comprehensive documentation examples Comprehensive test: Test valiting mathematical correctness

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
code: # Install from source devtools::install_github("alrobles/sobol")

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: common task explore high‑dimensional parameter region running optimiser. sobol_design() returns data frame scaled points can feed directly objective function. design fills space evenly random sampling – key advantage global optimization.
code: library(sobol)  # Define ranges for three fake hyperparameters design <- sobol_design(   lower = c(learning_rate = 0.0001, momentum = 0.00, dropout = 0.0),   upper = c(learning_rate = 0.1000, momentum = 0.99, dropout = 0.5),   nseq   = 200 )  head(design)

what: Going Deeper: Raw Points and Stateful Generation
title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: sobol_design() convenience layer built top two lower‑level backends. can use directly need raw (un‑scaled) points incremental control.

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
code: mat <- sobol_points(n = 512, dim = 4)   # 512×4 matrix in [0,1)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: generator perfect interleaved generation (generate points, evaluate, generate ) parallel scouts handle non‑overlapping segment sequence.
code: gen <- sobol_generator(dim = 2, skip = 1000) pt  <- sobol_next(gen)                  # vector of length 2 batch <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 100)     # 100×2 matrix  # Jump to any index (0‑based) sobol_skip_to(gen, 2048) sobol_index(gen)                        # current position

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Sobol sequences fully deterministic. Identical parameters always yield identical points. parallel optimisation multi‑node sampling, use skipping partition sequence: coordination workers – just choose non‑overlapping skip offsets.
code: # Worker 1 handles points 0–999 w1 <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dim = 3, skip = 0)  # Worker 2 handles points 1000–1999 w2 <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dim = 3, skip = 1000)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: library heavily optimised: Compiler intrinsics trailing‑zero count (GCC/Clang/MSVC) Column‑major layout direct R matrix integration Even 1 million points 10 dimensions execute second modern hardware.

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Generate matrix Sobol points one call:
code: a <- sobol_design(lower = c(p = 0), upper = c(p = 1), nseq = 64) b <- sobol_design(lower = c(p = 0), upper = c(p = 1), nseq = 64) identical(a, b)   # TRUE library(sobol)  # Generate 1000 points in 5 dimensions points <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dimensions = 5) dim(points)  # [1] 1000    5  # All values are in [0, 1) range(points)  # [1] 0.0000000 0.9999999

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Create generator object sequential point generation:
code: # Create a 3-dimensional generator gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 3) print(gen) # <Sobol Sequence Generator> #   Dimensions: 3 #   Initial skip: 0 #   Current index: 0 #   Points generated: 0  # Generate single points point1 <- sobol_next(gen) point2 <- sobol_next(gen) point3 <- sobol_next(gen)  # Generate multiple points at once batch <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 100)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Jump specific indices sequence:
code: # Start from index 1000 gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2, skip = 1000)  # Or skip later gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2) sobol_skip_to(gen, 1000) point <- sobol_next(gen)  # This is point #1000

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Check current state generator:
code: gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 3) sobol_next_n(gen, n = 50)  # Get current index sobol_index(gen)  # 50  # Get dimensions sobol_dimensions(gen)  # 3  # Get summary summary(gen) # Sobol Sequence Generator Summary # ================================ # Dimensions:        3 # Initial skip:      0 # Current index:     50 # Points generated:  50

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: package implements standard S3 methods sobol_generator class: print(): Display generator state summary(): Detailed summary generator

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: sobol_generator(dimensions, skip = 0): Create new Sobol generator

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: sobol_next(x): Generate next point sobol_next_n(x, n): Generate n points sobol_points(n, dimensions, skip = 0): Batch generation (stateless)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: sobol_skip_to(x, index): Jump specific index sobol_index(x): Get current index sobol_dimensions(x): Get number dimensions

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: Sobol sequences fully deterministic. Generators parameters always produce identical sequences: skip functionality enables reproducible subsequences parallel generation:
code: gen1 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2) gen2 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2)  points1 <- sobol_next_n(gen1, n = 100) points2 <- sobol_next_n(gen2, n = 100)  identical(points1, points2)  # TRUE # Generate points 100-199 worker1 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2, skip = 100) batch1 <- sobol_next_n(worker1, n = 100)  # Generate points 200-299 worker2 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2, skip = 200) batch2 <- sobol_next_n(worker2, n = 100)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
code: # Integrate a function over [0,1]^d integrate_mc <- function(f, dimensions, n_points) {   points <- sobol_points(n = n_points, dimensions = dimensions)   mean(apply(points, 1, f)) }  # Example: integrate f(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 f <- function(p) p[1]^2 + p[2]^2 result <- integrate_mc(f, dimensions = 2, n_points = 10000)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
code: # Generate parameter combinations for grid search params <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dimensions = 3)  # Map to actual parameter ranges learning_rate <- params[, 1] * 0.1  # [0, 0.1] momentum <- params[, 2] * 0.9 + 0.1  # [0.1, 1.0] dropout <- params[, 3] * 0.5  # [0, 0.5]

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
code: # Generate input samples for sensitivity analysis gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 10)  # Generate primary samples A <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 1000)  # Generate secondary samples B <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 1000)

title: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator
text: package uses header-C++ implementation Rcpp R integration. Batch generation highly optimized:
code: # Generate 1 million points in 10 dimensions system.time(sobol_points(n = 1e6, dimensions = 10)) #   user  system elapsed #  0.234   0.012   0.247

what: sobol: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol-package
title: sobol: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol-package
text: Provides fast efficient implementation Sobol sequences quasi-Monte Carlo methods. Sobol sequence low-discrepancy sequence property values N, subsequence x1, ..., xN low discrepancy. can used generate quasi-random numbers use Monte Carlo integration simulation methods. implementation based algorithms described Bratley Fox (1988) doi:10.1145/42288.214372  uses direction numbers Joe Kuo (2008) doi:10.1145/1358628.1358630 . package includes batch incremental interfaces support arbitrary starting indices reproducible sequences. uses 'Rcpp' efficient 'C++' integration.

title: sobol: Quasi-Monte Carlo Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol-package
text: Maintainer: Angel Robles .l.robles.fernandez@gmail.com contributors: Ilya M. Sobol (Original Sobol sequence algorithm) [contributor] Paul Bratley (Algorithm implementation reference) [contributor] Bennett L. Fox (Algorithm implementation reference) [contributor] Stephen Joe (Direction numbers primitive polynomials) [contributor] Frances Y. Kuo (Direction numbers primitive polynomials) [contributor]

what: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
text: Creates Latin hypercube design based Sobol low-discrepancy sequence. function provides API-compatible alternative sobol_design function pomp-explore package generating parameter designs.

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
code: sobol_design(lower = numeric(0), upper = numeric(0), nseq)

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
text: lower Named numeric vector giving lower bounds parameter ranges. Must names upper. upper Named numeric vector giving upper bounds parameter ranges. Must names lower. nseq Integer, total number parameter sets (points) generate.

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
text: data frame nseq rows one column parameter   named lower upper. column contains values scaled   specified range [lower, upper] parameter.

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
text: function generates Sobol sequence unit hypercube [0,1]^d scales dimension specified parameter ranges. Sobol sequence generated using Joe-Kuo direction numbers Property enforcement, providing excellent low-discrepancy properties. Following recommendation Joe & Kuo (2003) implementation pomp-explore, function skips first k points Sobol sequence, k largest power 2 smaller nseq. improves uniformity properties generated design. function designed API-compatible sobol_design function pomp-explore package, allowing easy comparison drop-replacement.

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
text: Bratley, P., & Fox, B. L. (1988). Algorithm 659: Implementing Sobol's quasirandom sequence generator. ACM Transactions Mathematical Software, 14(1), 88-100. Joe, S., & Kuo, F. Y. (2008). Constructing Sobol sequences better two-dimensional projections. SIAM Journal Scientific Computing, 30(5), 2635-2654.

title: Generate a Sobol Design for Parameter Exploration — sobol_design
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate 100 parameter sets for two parameters design <- sobol_design(   lower = c(a = 0, b = 100),   upper = c(a = 1, b = 200),   nseq = 100 ) head(design)  # Plot the design plot(design$a, design$b, main = "Sobol Design")  # High-dimensional example params <- paste0("param", 1:10) design_10d <- sobol_design(   lower = setNames(rep(0, 10), params),   upper = setNames(rep(1, 10), params),   nseq = 1000 ) } # }

what: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
title: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
text: Creates S3 object wraps Rcpp Sobol sequence generator incremental point generation. generator maintains internal state allows efficient generation quasi-random sequences.

title: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
code: sobol_generator(dimensions, skip = 0)

title: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
text: dimensions Integer, number dimensions Sobol sequence. Must positive integer. skip Numeric, number initial points skip (default: 0). allows starting sequence index reproducibility.

title: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
text: S3 object class "sobol_generator" containing: generator underlying Rcpp reference class object dimensions Number dimensions initial_skip Initial skip value used construction

title: Create a Sobol Sequence Generator — sobol_generator
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Create a 3-dimensional Sobol generator gen <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 3)  # Generate a single point point <- sobol_next(gen)  # Generate multiple points points <- sobol_next_n(gen, n = 100)  # Skip to a specific index sobol_skip_to(gen, 1000)  # Create a generator starting from index 50 gen2 <- sobol_generator(dimensions = 2, skip = 50) } # }

what: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
title: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
text: Efficiently generates matrix Sobol sequence points. convenience function maintain state calls. incremental generation, use sobol_generator instead.

title: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
text: n Integer, number points generate. Must non-negative. dim Integer, number dimensions point. Must positive integer. skip Numeric, number initial points skip (default: 0). allows generating subsequences Sobol sequence.

title: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
text: numeric matrix n rows dim columns,   row represents point Sobol sequence. Values [0, 1).   n = 0, returns 0 x dim matrix.

title: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
text: function implemented directly C++ via Rcpp efficient creating generator calling sobol_next_n know advance many points need. skip parameter allows start point sequence, useful : Reproducibility: generating subsequence across runs Parallelization: different workers can generate     non-overlapping segments Continuation: extending previous sequence without     regenerating early points

title: Generate a Batch of Sobol Points — sobol_points
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Generate 1000 points in 5 dimensions points <- sobol_points(n = 1000, dim = 5) dim(points) # [1] 1000    5  # Skip the first 100 points points_skipped <- sobol_points(n = 100, dim = 2, skip = 100)  # Empty result empty <- sobol_points(n = 0, dim = 3) dim(empty) # [1] 0 3 } # }
